title: Expressing support for the designation of the fourth Wednesday of February as "Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This bill wants to make the fourth Wednesday of February a special day to teach people about a heart disease called hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. This heart disease is passed down in families and affects hundreds of thousands of Americans. Most people who have this disease do not know they have it because they have never been tested for it. The bill wants to help people learn about this heart problem so they can go to the doctor and get the right medical care if they need it.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for making the fourth Wednesday of February a day called Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Day.

sections.title: Facts About Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
sections.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (called HCM for short) is a long-lasting heart disease where the heart muscle becomes too thick. This can cause serious problems including heart failure, irregular heartbeat, stroke, and in rare cases sudden death. HCM is the most common heart disease that parents can pass down to their children. It can affect anyone of any age, gender, or race. Between one in two hundred to one in five hundred people have this condition. Doctors estimate that between seven hundred thousand and one million six hundred fifty thousand people in the United States have HCM, but eighty-five percent of them do not know they have it. HCM causes symptoms like shortness of breath, chest pain, tiredness, feeling your heart beat irregularly, and fainting. These symptoms are similar to other heart and lung diseases, which makes HCM hard to diagnose. People with untreated HCM are three to four times more likely to die than people without the disease. Knowing your family medical history and recognizing symptoms is the first important step to getting the right diagnosis. Healthcare providers must ask detailed questions about heart health to find people who might have heart problems that run in families or are present from birth. Doctors may use several tests to diagnose HCM, including an echocardiogram (a test that uses sound waves to take pictures of the heart), a cardiac MRI (detailed pictures of the heart using magnets), or genetic testing to check for family history of HCM. After diagnosing HCM, patients should work with their healthcare providers to learn about their disease and understand different ways to manage it, including prescription medicines and surgery options that might help. Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Day is expected to be observed in the United States for many years to come, giving hope and information to patients, caregivers, and families across the country.

sections.title: House of Representatives Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Awareness Day an official day. The House recognizes that raising awareness about hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is very important for improving heart health. The House also recognizes the need to teach the public about the dangers of not diagnosing and treating hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and encourages all Americans to get proper medical care.
